questions.stem: Which of the following is not a protected characteristic under the Equality Act 2010?
options: Age
Sexual orientation
Political opinion
Religion or belief
questions.rationale: Section 4 of the Equality Act 2010 enumerates the nine protected characteristics. Political opinion is not one of them.

questions.stem: A colleague tells a joke about a national stereotype in the kitchen. Nobody of that nationality is present. Can this be harassment?
options: No, because nobody of that nationality heard it
Yes, if a colleague who heard it found it offensive and a reasonable person would agree
Only if the joke was directed at a specific person
Only if it is repeated multiple times
questions.rationale: Section 26 EA 2010 makes clear that conduct related to a protected characteristic can be harassment of any colleague who hears it, judged by purpose or effect.

questions.stem: Under the Equality Act, harassment is judged primarily by:
options: The intention of the person who made the comment or took the action
Whether the conduct was repeated more than once
The purpose or effect of the conduct, judged by what a reasonable person would conclude
Whether a formal complaint was made
questions.rationale: Section 26(4) EA 2010 tells tribunals to weigh the recipient's perception, the circumstances, and whether it is reasonable for the conduct to have that effect. Intent is irrelevant.

questions.stem: [scenario] At a Q3 management committee, your COO proposes leaving the firm's preventative-duty training as it is until 'we see what October 2026 actually requires.' What is the legally accurate response?
options: Wait — the new standard is not in force yet, so the existing material is sufficient.

The s.40A duty has been live since October 2024 already, and from 1 October 2026 the standard rises to 'all reasonable steps' (s.20 ERA 2025) with third-party liability added (s.21). Delay is itself evidence of failure to take reasonable steps.
Wait — the EHRC publishes detailed regulations in 2027 and the firm should defer until then.
The duty applies only to firms with more than 250 employees; the rest can wait.
questions.rationale: Section 40A is already in force (since 26 October 2024). From 1 October 2026 the standard rises to 'all reasonable steps' via s.20 ERA 2025, and s.21 inserts third-party liability via new s.40(1A)–(1C) EA 2010. The EHRC Technical Guidance is the operative reference until s.22 regulations land in 2027. The duty has no size threshold. Sitting on existing material is itself a fact tribunals will weigh.

questions.stem: The maximum uplift an employment tribunal can apply to a successful sexual harassment compensation award where the employer has not met its preventative duty is:
questions.rationale: Section 124A EA 2010 (inserted by the Worker Protection Act 2023) allows an uplift of up to 25%.

questions.stem: A senior manager repeatedly compliments a junior colleague on her appearance during team meetings. She has not complained, but visibly looks uncomfortable. Is this potentially harassment?
options: No, because she hasn't complained
No, because compliments are not harassment
Yes, if a reasonable person would consider the conduct has the effect of violating her dignity or creating an uncomfortable environment
Only if he does it again after she objects
questions.rationale: The purpose-or-effect test in s.26(1) EA 2010 doesn't require a complaint — it asks what a reasonable person would conclude about the effect.

questions.stem: Which statement about sexual harassment is correct?
options: It can only occur between people of different sexes
It requires physical contact
It can occur between any combination of people regardless of gender
It only applies to conduct outside the office
questions.rationale: Section 26(2) EA 2010 is gender-neutral and not limited to physical conduct.

questions.stem: A single serious incident:
options: Cannot constitute harassment because harassment requires a pattern
Can constitute harassment on its own if sufficiently serious
Only counts if witnessed by others
Only counts if the recipient reports it within 48 hours
questions.rationale: The Equality Act does not require a pattern of behaviour — a single sufficiently serious incident can meet the s.26 test.

questions.stem: Victimisation under the Equality Act means:
options: Being the victim of harassment
Being treated badly because you have made or supported a complaint about discrimination or harassment
Being unfairly criticised at work
Being passed over for promotion
questions.rationale: Section 27 EA 2010 defines victimisation as detriment because the person has done a 'protected act' (made or supported a complaint).

questions.stem: A client makes a sexual comment to a member of our reception team during a visit. From October 2026, what is the firm's potential exposure?
options: None, because the harasser is not an employee
The firm could be liable for failing to take all reasonable steps to prevent third-party harassment
The firm is only liable if the same client offends multiple times
The firm is liable only if the receptionist is physically harmed
questions.rationale: Section 21 of the Employment Rights Act 2025 reinstates third-party harassment liability from October 2026, across all protected characteristics.

questions.stem: Which of the following can constitute sexual harassment?
options: Persistent requests for dates after refusal
Sharing sexually explicit images
Inappropriate questions about someone's sex life
All of the above
questions.rationale: Section 26(2) covers unwanted conduct of a sexual nature in any form.

questions.stem: In the financial services context, harassment by an SMCR-certified individual:
options: Is purely an HR matter
Can affect that individual's fitness and propriety and the firm's regulatory standing
Is only relevant if it occurs in the office
Only matters if the recipient is also SMCR-certified
questions.rationale: From 1 September 2026 COCON 1.1.7FR brings harassment into the Conduct Rules for every SMCR firm; FIT 2.1 / PS25/23 confirm a substantiated finding is relevant to fitness and propriety.

questions.stem: Two colleagues are dating. The relationship ends. One of them continues to send unsolicited messages of a personal nature to the other through Teams. The recipient asks them to stop, but the messages continue. This is most accurately described as:
options: A private matter between two adults
Potential sexual harassment in the workplace
Not a workplace issue because it is via Teams
Only a workplace issue if the messages are explicit
questions.rationale: Workplace channels are the workplace. Unwanted persistent contact of a personal nature after refusal can be sexual harassment under s.26(2).

questions.stem: [scenario] After a long deal-closing dinner a senior analyst makes a comment about a colleague's accent that several at the table find off-key. The next morning they apologise on a Teams DM with 'it was just banter, mate, you know how I am.' Which is the legally accurate read?
options: The apology resolves the matter.
Because intent was not to offend, there is no harassment in law.

Section 26 judges harassment by purpose or effect — 'it was just banter' is not a defence. The apology is a piece of evidence (and not necessarily a helpful one if it documents that the speaker noticed the impact).
The conduct happened outside the office so the Equality Act does not apply.
questions.rationale: s.26(1) judges harassment by purpose or effect. Intent is irrelevant. An apology may mitigate but does not undo the conduct. Course-of-employment extends to work-adjacent settings such as client dinners (s.109 EA 2010 vicarious liability).

questions.stem: The Equality Act protects against harassment related to:
options: Sex only
Sex and race only
Any of the seven harassment-relevant protected characteristics (age, disability, gender reassignment, race, religion or belief, sex, sexual orientation)
Any characteristic the recipient finds important
questions.rationale: Section 26 covers conduct related to seven of the nine — marriage/civil partnership and pregnancy/maternity sit outside s.26 but remain protected against direct discrimination under s.13 / s.18.

questions.stem: A manager passes a team member over for a high-profile project shortly after that team member declined an invitation to dinner. The team member believes the two are linked. Under the Equality Act, this is potentially:
options: Lawful, because project allocation is a management decision
Sexual harassment through less favourable treatment for rejecting sexual conduct
Only actionable if there is written evidence linking the two
A matter for the manager's discretion
questions.rationale: Section 26(3) EA 2010 captures less favourable treatment for rejecting or submitting to sexual conduct, even where the underlying conduct itself might not meet the s.26(1) bar.

questions.stem: Disclosures relating to sexual harassment, as of April 2026, are automatically classified as:
options: Internal grievances only
Protected disclosures under whistleblowing law
Confidential HR matters with no legal status
Matters for the police
questions.rationale: Section 23 ERA 2025 (commenced 6 April 2026) inserts a new paragraph into s.43B ERA 1996 making sexual-harassment disclosures protected disclosures.

questions.stem: Indirect discrimination occurs when:
options: Someone is treated badly directly because of a protected characteristic
A policy that applies to everyone disadvantages people with a particular protected characteristic without justification
Someone overhears a discriminatory remark
An employer fails to provide training
questions.rationale: Section 19 EA 2010 defines indirect discrimination as a 'provision, criterion or practice' that puts people with a protected characteristic at a disadvantage without objective justification.

questions.stem: At a Friday team drinks, a member of the team makes a comment about a colleague's religious dress. The colleague laughs along but later tells you privately that she was hurt. Is this potentially harassment?
options: No, because she laughed at the time
No, because it was outside the office
Yes; the conduct relates to religion and may have had the effect of violating her dignity regardless of her outward reaction
Only if she lodges a formal complaint
questions.rationale: Course-of-employment extends to work-adjacent social events; outward reaction does not determine the s.26 purpose-or-effect test.

questions.stem: Which of these is not one of the four main types of prohibited conduct under the Equality Act?
options: Direct discrimination
Indirect discrimination
Constructive misconduct
Victimisation
questions.rationale: The four are direct, indirect, harassment, and victimisation. 'Constructive misconduct' is not a recognised category.

questions.stem: The body responsible for enforcing the Equality Act at a regulatory level is:
options: ACAS
The Equality and Human Rights Commission (EHRC)
The Financial Conduct Authority
HMRC
questions.rationale: The EHRC has investigatory and enforcement powers under the Equality Act 2006.

questions.stem: During a client lunch, a client makes a derogatory comment about a particular ethnic group. Two of our staff are at the table. Neither is from that ethnic group. Can this still be harassment of our staff?
options: No, because the comment wasn't about them
No, because the client is not an employee
Yes; harassment can occur where conduct related to a protected characteristic creates a hostile environment, even if not directed at the recipient
Only if a staff member is from that group
questions.rationale: Section 26 captures conduct 'related to' a protected characteristic; from October 2026, s.21 ERA 2025 makes the firm directly liable for third-party harassment of its employees.

questions.stem: The 'all reasonable steps' defence requires an employer to demonstrate:
options: That a written policy exists
That training has been delivered at some point
That a comprehensive set of preventative measures — including risk assessment, policies, reporting routes, and training — has been implemented and monitored
That no complaint has previously been received
questions.rationale: The EHRC Technical Guidance and 8-step employer guide treat the test as the combination of measures, not any single one.

questions.stem: A colleague asks you out. You decline politely. They ask again the following week. You decline again, more firmly. They ask a third time. Is this potentially harassment?
options: No, persistence is flattering
No, asking someone out is never harassment
Yes; persistent unwanted conduct of a sexual or romantic nature after clear refusal can constitute sexual harassment
Only if they touch you
questions.rationale: Persistent unwanted requests after clear refusal can meet the s.26(2) sexual-harassment test.

questions.stem: The key reason this training matters to you personally, beyond the firm's legal duty, is:
options: It helps avoid awkward conversations
It is mandatory
Harassment causes serious harm to colleagues, can have regulatory consequences for individuals in financial services, and creates personal liability in some cases
It improves team productivity
questions.rationale: The training combines the duty-of-care to colleagues with the personal regulatory exposure under SMCR (COCON 1.1.7FR, FIT 2.1, SYSC 22 references).

reflections.prompt: Looking at the seven harassment-relevant protected characteristics, which one do you think colleagues at the firm are least likely to recognise as a basis for harassment?
reflections.guidance: Logged but not scored. Your line manager will never see this — only Group HR, and only as anonymised aggregate themes.

reflections.prompt: If you witnessed something this week that fell into the 'purpose or effect' grey area, would you raise it? With whom — and if not, what would stop you?
reflections.guidance: Logged but not scored. Your line manager will never see this — only Group HR, and only as anonymised aggregate themes.
